title: Maclura thorelii
intro: Maclura thorelii, also known as the Cambodian hackberry, is a species of deciduous tree native to Southeast Asia. The tree has been traditionally used in folk medicine, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in reducing blood sugar levels and improving cardiovascular health. The bioactive constituents of Maclura thorelii include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Maclura thorelii is often used to prepare decoctions, infusions, and extracts, as well as being used in the manufacture of herbal teas and capsules.
taxonomy: Maclura thorelii is classified in the kingdom Plantae, which further divides into the phylum Angiospermophyta, class Rosopsida, order Rosales, family Moraceae, and genus Maclura. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and evolutionary relationships. The family Moraceae is commonly known as the mulberry or fig family, which includes other species like the white mulberry (Morus alba). Maclura thorelii is a unique species within this family, distinguished by its distinct morphology and growth habits. As a member of the Rosales order, Maclura thorelii is also related to other plant species with similar characteristics, such as the apple and rose families.
uses: Maclura thorelii is a species of plant that is native to Vietnam and southern China. Although there is limited information available on its medicinal uses, some studies suggest that extracts from this plant may have potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.
